coredata.dc:title: Utilizing copper-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for determination of carbamazepine in exhaled breath condensate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Keyvani, Aylar
Jouyban-Gharamaleki, Vahid
Khoubnasabjafari, Maryam
Soleymani, Jafar
Rahimpour, Elaheh
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: Carbamazepine is a versatile pharmaceutical used mainly for epilepsy and mood stabilization, but like all medications, it requires careful monitoring due to its potential side effects and interactions. In the current work, a fluorescent nanosensor was developed for the carbamazepine determination in the exhaled breath condensate (EBC). The nanosensor consists of graphene quantum dots doped with copper (Cu-doped GQDs). The presence of carbamazepine significantly decreased the fluorescence of Cu-doped GQDs, resulting in a quenching effect within a concentration range of 0.02–2.0 µg/mL, which occurs due to dynamic, rather than static, quenching according to Stern–Volmer plot. The relative standard deviations for intra-day and inter-day analysis of carbamazepine were 1.8 % and 4.8 %, respectively. The validated sensor was effectively employed for carbamazepine analysis in EBC of patients receiving the drug.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbamazepine
Fluorescent sensor
Copper-doped carbon quantum dots
Exhaled breath condensate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Research Affairs of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) under the grant number 74337.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization study: (A) Excitation and emission spectrum (50 μL of Cu-doped GQDs in 0.5 mL water), (B) TEM image (C) DLS (D) EDX (E) ATR-FT-IR and (F) XRD and (G) stability analyses of Cu-doped GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the Cu-doped GQDs sensor in the absence and presence of different concentrations of carbamazepine: (0.02–2.0 µg/mL) in the EBC sample. Inset: calibration curve. Conditions: pH 5.0, a Cu-doped GQDs volume of 50 μL, an immediate measurement with λex = 380 nm. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the un-doped GQDs in the absence (I) and presence (II) of carbamazepine 1.0 µg/mL.
Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of Cu-doped GQDs sensor by carbamazepine.
Optimization procedure for the study of the effect of (A) pH, (B) Cu-doped GQDs volume, (C) incubation time and (D) temperature on the sensor response.

The effect of some possible available over-the-counter or co-administrated drugs as interference substances on the probe response. Carbamazepine concentration was 0.5 µg/mL and the interfering pharmaceuticals were 1.0 μg/mL.
General layout for synthesizing Cu-doped GQDs, carbamazepine determination, and the possible interaction between the Cu-doped GQDs and carbamazepine.
Comparison of analytical characteristics of the developed method with other literature methods for the determination of carbamazepine.
Carbamazepine analysis using the validated sensor in patient’s EBC samples.

head.ce:title: Utilizing copper-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for determination of carbamazepine in exhaled breath condensate

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Anesthesiology and Intensive Care, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbamazepine is a versatile pharmaceutical used mainly for epilepsy and mood stabilization, but like all medications, it requires careful monitoring due to its potential side effects and interactions. In the current work, a fluorescent nanosensor was developed for the carbamazepine determination in the exhaled breath condensate (EBC). The nanosensor consists of graphene quantum dots doped with copper (Cu-doped GQDs). The presence of carbamazepine significantly decreased the fluorescence of Cu-doped GQDs, resulting in a quenching effect within a concentration range of 0.02–2.0 µg/mL, which occurs due to dynamic, rather than static, quenching according to Stern–Volmer plot. The relative standard deviations for intra-day and inter-day analysis of carbamazepine were 1.8 % and 4.8 %, respectively. The validated sensor was effectively employed for carbamazepine analysis in EBC of patients receiving the drug.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbamazepine is prescribed for the management of epilepsy and pain linked to true trigeminal neuralgia. It has proven effective in treating various seizure types, including mixed seizures, partial seizures with complex symptoms, and generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Additionally, carbamazepine is indicated for addressing manic episodes and mixed manic-depressive episodes associated with bipolar I disorder [1]. As a gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) agonist, carbamazepine promotes the activation of GABA receptors, resulting in a sedative effect. Its mechanism of action involves the inhibition of the alpha subunit of voltage-gated sodium channels [2]. The therapeutic concentration range for this drug in plasma and serum samples is 2.0–12 μg/mL [3]. Frequent side effects of carbamazepine are dizziness, drowsiness, unsteadiness while walking, and feelings of nausea or vomiting. Severe adverse reactions may occur, such as serious skin reactions (e.g., Stevens-Johnson syndrome), blood disorders, liver damage, allergic reactions, changes in heart rate, hyponatremia, suicidal thoughts, fever, shortness of breath, pale or yellowish skin, easy bruising or bleeding, frequent infections, unusual weakness or tiredness, lightheadedness, and headaches. Additionally, due to its mild anticholinergic properties, carbamazepine may elevate the risk of delirium in older adults, potentially leading to urinary retention, increased intraocular pressure, and constipation [4]. As a result, monitoring its levels in body fluids is vital for personalizing the dosage and ensuring that therapeutic levels remain effective.

Several techniques have been reported for carbamazepine determining in pharmaceutical and biological samples, including spectrophotometry [5,6], spectrofluorimetry [7,8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-UV [9], ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) [10], capillary electrophoresis [11], potentiometry [12] and voltammetry [13]. While the existing methods have their advantages and disadvantages, creating a new, user-friendly approach for measuring drug concentrations in biological samples remains a significant challenge for clinicians and researchers. Fluorescence-based optical techniques are favored for their ease of use, responsiveness, and low cost in point-of-care applications. However, this method faces issues with sensitivity and selectivity, which can be compensated through nanomaterials. Recent developments in nanotechnology have led to the development of nanomaterials that possess enhanced or altered physicochemical properties, such as controlled fluorescence, high yield, stability, good solubility, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. These properties led to their increasing applicability and popularity in sensor design [14,15].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well-known nanomaterial and nanoscale fragments of graphene that exhibit distinctive optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement effects. These properties arise from the limited size and dimensionality of the material, which leads to a significant change in its electronic behavior compared to bulk graphene. Key characteristics of GQDs include strong photoluminescence, which can be tuned depending on their size and surface functionalization, making them valuable for various optical [16] and clinical and environmental sensing applications [17–19]. Additionally, GQDs possess excellent electrical conductivity, thermal stability, and a high surface area, which enhance their interaction with other materials. Their surface chemistry is highly adaptable, allowing for the introduction of functional groups or doping with heteroatoms that can improve biocompatibility and enable conjugation with biomolecules for specific applications in sensing/biosensing and drug delivery. It is important to note that modification of GQDs improves their properties such as fluorescence, boosting their quantum yield and selectivity and broadening their potential applications [20].

Despite the growing interest in this research field, designing a reliable method for drug tracing in biological samples is a critical knowledge gap. The aim and novelty of this work is lie in leveraging the specific interaction between carbamazepine and copper to create a simple and efficient sensor using copper-doped graphene quantum dots (Cu-doped GQDs) for detecting and quantifying carbamazepine in exhaled breath condensate (EBC) samples collected from patients undergoing carbamazepine treatment. A schematic illustration describing the whole procedure was shown in Scheme 1. EBC is a biological sample consisting of condensed tiny droplets of lung lining fluid exhaled during normal breathing [21]. It contains fewer interfering substances compared to other matrices such as blood, plasma, urine, and sputum, facilitating its direct analysis.

Citric acid, hydrochloric acid (HCl) sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Cu(NO3)2.3H2O and NaBH4 were provided from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Carbamazepine was purchased from Arastoo Pharmaceutical Company (Tehran, Iran). A carbamazepine solution in a concentration of 1 g/L was prepared by dissolving the required value of drug powder in ethanol. The solution was then reserved in a refrigerator at a 4 °C, away from light. The working solutions were obtained by properly diluting the standard solution with deionized water.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Jasco FP 750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan), which utilized a xenon lamp for excitation. A standard 0.7 mL quartz cell was employed to record the spectral data, with bandwidths set to 5 nm for excitation and emission paths. The pH was adjusted by a digital pH meter (model 744 from Metrohm Ltd, Herisau, Switzerland). Additionally, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a Philips (Eindhoven, The Netherlands) instrument, to obtain the shape and size of the synthesized nanoparticles. Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed using a MIRA3 FEG-SEM from Tescan (Brno, Czech Republic). The produced nanoprobe's particle size distribution and zeta potential measurements were estimated using dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis by a Microtrac® Inc. DLS Nanotrac® instrument. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were analyzed with a Siemens diffractometer (Tongda Co., China) utilizing filtered Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV across a 2θ range of 10° to 60°. Attenuated Total Reflectance-Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FT-IR) spectroscopy was also performed using the shimadzu model 1601 (Japon), to verify the chemical bonding of the nanoparticles. Moreover, EBC samples were obtained from a healthy volunteer using a lab-made setup [22].

GQDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis of citric acid using a published method [23], with some modifications. In summary, 2.0 g of citric acid was placed in a beaker and heated to 230 ºC using a heating mantle until it transformed into an orange liquid. Then, a 30 mL solution of 10 mg/mL NaOH was added dropwise to the orange liquid while stirring vigorously and continuously. The resulting GQD was kept in the refrigerator in a dark location.

Subsequently, the Cu-doped GQDs were synthesized by slowly adding a 2 mL solution of Cu(NO3)2 (0.02 mol/L) dropwise to a 15 mL solution of GQDs, while stirring vigorously with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature (25 ºC). After approximately 1 h of stirring, 0.4 mL of a solution of 0.05 mol/L NaBH4 (to reduce copper ions for doping the GQDs) in deionized water was introduced to the mixture, and stirring continued strongly for about 60 min [24].

A lab-made device was employed to collect EBC samples [22]. This device is equipped with a cooling trap that allows for temperature regulation between 0 °C and −25 °C. It quickly cools the air, condenses the water vapor, and extracts particles from the trap's surface, facilitating direct analysis without further treatment. Samples were collected from healthy volunteers for method optimization and validation. For the real sample study, subjects were chosen from patients receiving carbamazepine. Written informed consent was obtained from each sample donor, and the project was approved by the Ethics Committee of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences under the code IR.TBZMED.REC.1403.484.

An analysis was conducted in a 2 mL microtube. The 10 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 5.0, 0.1 mol·L−1) and 100 μL of EBC spiked with different concentrations of carbamazepine were added into the microtube and then, 50 μL of Cu-doped GQDs was added, and the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 0.5 mL with deionized water. The fluorescence intensity was immediately measured at approximately λmax = 486 nm after excitation at 380 nm. The analytical response in the current work was the difference between the sensor signal in the presence and absence of carbamazepine, i.e., ΔF (F–F
0 ), where F represents the fluorescence intensity of the sensor with carbamazepine present, and F
0 represents the intensity in its absence. All measurements were performed in triplicate at room temperature (25 ºC).

Cu-doped GQDs were synthesized using a pyrolysis method. The synthesized nanomaterials displayed a strong fluorescence peak at 486 nm with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm ( Fig. 1A). The size and morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials were investigated using TEM analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, the Cu-doped GQDs appeared to have nearly uniform spherical shapes, with individual particle sizes under 10 nm, consistent with the DLS analysis presented in Fig. 1C and corroborated by prior literature reports [24]. Furthermore, the successful synthesis of Cu-doped GQDs was confirmed through EDX analysis, which indicated the chemical composition. Fig. 1D illustrates the distribution of elements such as C, O, and Cu, providing definitive evidence of the successful formation of the sensor. Furthermore, ATR-FT-IR was used to study the functional groups on the surface of Cu-doped GQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 1E, obvious absorption bands at 3000 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of C-H, and O-H. The peak at 1637 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the COO. These findings confirmed the existence of -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of quantum dots. The characteristic infrared absorption peak band of the Cu-O stretching vibration at about 665 cm−1 indicating the successful formation of Cu-doped GQDs [24]. To confirm the crystalline structure of the nanoparticles XRD analysis was performed on the synthesized samples (Fig. 1F). The XRD pattern of the Cu-doped GQDs showed distinct peaks at 2θ angle of 25°, which correspond to the (002) lattice planes of the GQDs. To explore the surface charge of the Cu-doped GQDs, surface zeta-potential was measured by DLS technique, which provide a negative value of −0.28 mv.

It should be noted that a stability study of synthesized nanoparticles was also conducted, and as you can see in Fig. 1G, the intensity of the synthesized nanoparticles remained stable for about a month, and then it smoothly decreased.

After the successful synthesis of Cu-doped GQDs, its performance was evaluated by adding various concentrations of carbamazepine to explore the interactions between the analyte and the nanosensor. The findings revealed that the nanosensor displayed a characteristic peak at 486 nm; upon the addition of carbamazepine, the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor diminished, suggesting an interaction between carbamazepine and the Cu-doped GQDs ( Fig. 2 A). The quenching of the fluorescence emission of the Cu-doped GQDs occurs due to the transfer of electronic energy from the excited state of the GQDs to the carbamazepine molecules, or through collisional quenching where the drug molecules interact with the excited GQDs, dissipating the energy as heat rather than light. This process is due to specific interaction between Cu ions and functional groups in carbamazepine, as reported in the previous works [8], Cu ions can form coordination complexes with the functional groups in carbamazepine, such as amine and carbonyl groups. This binding alters the electronic environment of the Cu-GQDs, affecting their electronic properties and stability. Additionally, the interaction may facilitate charge transfer between the Cu-GQDs and carbamazepine and lead to a redistribution of charge, which may enhance the non-radiative decay pathways, thereby reducing fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the binding of carbamazepine to Cu-GQDs can modify the energy levels of the excited states, making it easier for the excited electrons to return to the ground state without emitting photons, thus quenching the fluorescence. It should be noted that GQDs do not exhibit significant changes in their optical response without doping with copper in the presence of carbamazepine, according to Fig. 2B. The primary reason for doping with copper is using its specific interaction with carbamazepine, which ultimately affects the electronic transfer processes and leads to a reduction in fluorescence peak intensity.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to analyze the temperature-dependent, steady-state quenching of the Cu-doped GQDs sensor by carbamazepine.
(1)
I
0
I
f
= 1 +
K
SV
carbamazepine
Where I0 and If are the fluorescence intensities of the sensor in the absence and presence of carbamazepine, respectively. KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant that shows the efficacy of carbamazepine-induced fluorescence quenching [25]. As shown in Fig. 3, a linear relationship with increasing carbamazepine concentration was observed, implying binding will occur via a single type of quenching (dynamic or static). The increase in Ksv constants was approximately linear with increasing temperature. This observation implies dynamic, rather than static, quenching of carbamazepine.

In the optimization step, the effect of some essential variables, including pH, the Cu-doped GQDs volume, and reaction time were investigated to optimize the analytical response of the system for carbamazepine analysis. A carbamazepine concentration of 0.5 µg/mL was selected for the optimization procedure. At first, the pH effect from 3.0 to 9.0 on the sensor's response was investigated ( Fig. 4A). The results indicated that the system exhibited the strongest response at pH 5.0, related to the high affinity of copper ions in the structure of in relatively acidic conditions to carbamazepine [26]. Thus, pH 5.0 was chosen as the optimal pH for subsequent experiments. Next, the effect of the Cu-doped GQDs volume on the analytical response was tested within the range of 10–75 µL (Fig. 4B). The results showed that the ΔF increased up to 50 µL and reached a plateau in a high volume of nanoparticles. Therefore, 50 µL was the optimal volume for future experiments. Lastly, the reaction time was checked over a range of 0–20 min, with the system showing a constant response of up to 20 min (Fig. 4C). It is worth noting that temperature can also affect fluorescence response, and herein this effect was investigated in the range of 5–45 °C. The optimal reaction conditions were found to occur between 5 and 30 °C (Fig. 4D). Therefore, all following experiments were conducted at room temperature (25 ºC). Briefly, the optimized reaction conditions were: pH 5.0, a Cu-doped GQDs volume of 50 μL, and an immediate measurement at room temperature (25 ºC).

To evaluate the analytical performance of the proposed method for measuring carbamazepine in EBC samples, we reported the calibration curve, limit of detection (LOD), and precision under optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the calibration curve showed a strong linear relationship for carbamazepine across the concentration range of 0.02–2.0 µg/mL. The equation for the regression line was as ΔF = 158.01 C
CBZ + 10.875. In this equation, ΔF is the difference in fluorescence intensity when carbamazepine is absent versus when it is present, while C
CBZ represents the carbamazepine concentration in µg/mL. The LOD is defined as 3S
b /m, where S
b shows the standard deviation of the blank and m denotes the slope of the calibration plot. The LOD for the developed method was found to be 0.004 µg/mL. The results from five replicate analyses of 0.5 µg/mL carbamazepine performed on the same day and on different days yielded relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.8 % and 4.8 %, respectively. To assess the effectiveness of this method in comparison to other previously reported techniques for carbamazepine analysis, we collected and presented relevant data from the literature in Table 1. As can be seen, the validated method in the current work demonstrated exceptional and comparable analytical features relative to these other approaches.

To investigate the selectivity of the developed sensor, the analysis was performed in the presence of various over-the-counter or co-administered drugs that could potentially interfere. The study investigated the effects of some of drugs, such as acetaminophen, aspirin, chlordiazepoxide, clonazepam, diazepam, nicotinamide, phenobarbital, lamotrigine, mefenamic acid, ibuprofen, naproxen, pantoprazole, phenytoin and sodium valproate at a concentration twice that of the analyte (carbamazepine set at 0.5 μg/mL and the interfering agents at 1.0 μg/mL) on the response of system toward carbamazepine under optimal conditions. This indicates that both the analyte and one of the interfering substances were present simultaneously in the solution being examined, allowing for the measurement of the interference's impact on the response. As illustrated in Fig. 5, most of the substances tested did not significantly affect the response, indicating the validated sensor possesses sufficient selectivity for carbamazepine analysis in real samples.

The validated sensor's performance for analyzing carbamazepine was investigated by analyzing four EBC samples collected from patients undergoing carbamazepine treatment. The results of this analysis were summarized in Table 2. To remove any potential matrix effects, a standard addition method was used to correct for sample matrix interferences, ensuring precise measurements. The analysis indicated that the concentrations of carbamazepine in the patient samples varied between 0.16 and 0.20 μg/mL.

The present study describes a fluorescent nanosensor that uses Cu-doped GQDs for the rapid and accurate detection of carbamazepine in EBC samples. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor diminished in a concentration-dependent manner as the carbamazepine concentration in the samples increased. A significant linear correlation was identified between fluorescence intensity and carbamazepine concentration, demonstrating a strong relationship between 0.02 and 2.0 µg/mL with LOD of 0.004 µg/mL. The method displayed excellent precision with low intra-day and inter-day RSD percentages of 1.8 % and 4.8 %, respectively. These results highlight the capability of the Cu-doped GQDs sensor to detect carbamazepine with high precision, and accuracy. Compared to other reliable methods like chromatography, this method offers several advantages, including faster response times, more straightforward sample preparation, user-friendly operations, and cost-effectiveness. Its acceptable performance makes it particularly well-suited for therapeutic drug monitoring, where precise quantification of drugs is critical to avoiding toxicity or subtherapeutic levels. In summary, the Cu-doped GQDs-based approach showed great potential for the analysis of carbamazepine in clinical settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethical approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: All sample donors or participants filled out and signed the informed consent of project with ethical code IR.TBZMED.REC.1403.484 confirmed by the ethics committee at Tabriz University of Medical Sciences verified.
This work was supported by the Research Affairs of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) under the grant number 74337.

Soleymani Jafar: Data curation. Khoubnasabjafari Maryam: Validation. Jouyban Abolghasem: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Rahimpour Elaheh: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jouyban-Gharamaleki Vahid: Validation. Keyvani Aylar: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current role of carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine in the management of epilepsy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Beydoun, S. DuPont, D. Zhou, M. Matta, V. Nagire, L. Lagae, Current role of carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine in the management of epilepsy, Seizure 83 (2020) 251-263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbamazepine, Chpatre 3 in "Profiles of drug substances, excipients and related"

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Alrashood, Carbamazepine, Chpatre 3 in "Profiles of drug substances, excipients and related" methodology 41 (2016) 133-321.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drug levels: therapeutic and toxic serum/plasma concentrations of common drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Regenthal, M. Krueger, C. Koeppel, R. Preiss, Drug levels: therapeutic and toxic serum/plasma concentrations of common drugs, J. Clin. Monit. Comput. 15 (1999) 529-544.
K.S. Walia, E.A. Khan, D.H. Ko, S.S. Raza, Y.N. Khan, Side effects of antiepileptics—a review, Pain Pract. 4 (2004) 194-203.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric and high performance liquid chromatographic determination of carbamazepine in tablets dosage form

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Bulduk, S. Gungor, Spectrophotometric and high performance liquid chromatographic determination of carbamazepine in tablets dosage form, J. Pharm. Res. Int. 33 (2021) 731-741.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and validation of UV–visible spectroscopic method for estimation of carbamazepine in bulk and tablet dosage form

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Zadbuke, S. Shahi, A. Jadhav, S. Borde, Development and validation of UV–visible spectroscopic method for estimation of carbamazepine in bulk and tablet dosage form, Int J Pharm Pharm Sci 8 (2016) 234-238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination and degradation of carbamazepine using g-C3N4@ CuS nanocomposite as sensitive fluorescence sensor and efficient photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Goudarzy, J. Zolgharnein, J.B. Ghasemi, Determination and degradation of carbamazepine using g-C3N4@ CuS nanocomposite as sensitive fluorescence sensor and efficient photocatalyst, Inorg. Chem. Commun. 141 (2022) 109512.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A single-shot diagnostic platform based on copper nanoclusters coated with cetyl trimethylammonium bromide for determination of carbamazepine in exhaled breath condensate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Hatefi, E. Rahimpour, M. Khoubnasabjafari, M. Edalat, V. Jouyban-Gharamaleki, S. Alvani-Alamdari, A. Nokhodchi, M.H. Pournaghi-Azar, A. Jouyban, A single-shot diagnostic platform based on copper nanoclusters coated with cetyl trimethylammonium bromide for determination of carbamazepine in exhaled breath condensate, Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 1-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of carbamazepine in urine and water samples using amino-functionalized metal–organic framework as sorbent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Rezaei Kahkha, A.R. Oveisi, M. Kaykhaii, B. Rezaei Kahkha, Determination of carbamazepine in urine and water samples using amino-functionalized metal–organic framework as sorbent, Chem. Cent. J. (2018) 1-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UHPLC-MS/MS method for simultaneous determination of carbamazepine and its seven major metabolites in serum of epileptic patients

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Jiang, T. Xia, Y. Yun, M. Li, F. Zhang, S. Gao, W. Chen, UHPLC-MS/MS method for simultaneous determination of carbamazepine and its seven major metabolites in serum of epileptic patients, J. Chromatogr. B 1108 (2019) 17-24.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper-based molecularly imprinted-interpenetrating polymer network for on-spot collection and microextraction of dried blood spots for capillary electrophoresis determination of carbamazepine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nuchtavorn, M. Dvořák, P. Kubáň, Paper-based molecularly imprinted-interpenetrating polymer network for on-spot collection and microextraction of dried blood spots for capillary electrophoresis determination of carbamazepine, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 412 (2020) 2721-2730.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a potentiometric sensor for the determination of carbamazepine and its application in pharmaceutical formulations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cetin, O. Özbek, A. Erol, Development of a potentiometric sensor for the determination of carbamazepine and its application in pharmaceutical formulations, J. Chem. Technol. Biotechnol. 98 (2023) 890-897.
